text: Balanced (平和质) constitution: the healthy baseline — good energy and appetite, radiant skin, sound sleep, adapts well to weather. Tongue: pale-red body with a thin white coating. Keep it with moderate regular meals, whole foods, steady activity (walking, Tai Chi), avoiding extremes and greasy food.

Qi-deficiency (气虚质) constitution: low energy and stamina — soft-spoken, easily out of breath, tires quickly, catches colds often, sweats easily. Tongue: pale body, often SWOLLEN with TOOTH-MARKS on the edges, thin coat. Supported by qi-nourishing warm foods (rice, mushrooms, dates, chicken), gentle exercise, and not overexerting.

Yang-deficiency (阳虚质) constitution: cold type — cold hands and feet, dislikes cold, worse after cold food/drink, low warmth. Tongue: pale, SWOLLEN, WET/moist, thin white coat. Supported by warming foods (ginger, garlic, beef), keeping the belly/lower back/feet warm, avoiding raw-cold food and long air-conditioning.

Yin-deficiency (阴虚质) constitution: dry-hot type — dry mouth/throat, thirst for cold drinks, warm palms and soles, night sweats, restless, often lean. Tongue: RED body, LITTLE or PEELED coating, often CRACKS. Supported by cooling-moistening foods (pear, duck, sea cucumber, fungus), good hydration, not over-sweating in exercise.

Phlegm-dampness (痰湿质) constitution: damp-heavy type — tends to overweight, heavy limbs, sticky mouth, dull complexion, likes greasy/rich food. Tongue: SWOLLEN with a THICK GREASY WHITE coating. Supported by draining foods (barley, red bean, millet, radish, celery), less fatty/sweet food, regular movement to 'move' damp.

Damp-heat (湿热质) constitution: hot-humid type — oily skin, acne, bitter taste, thirst, body heaviness, sticky/irregular stools, irritable; worse in humid weather. Tongue: RED body with a GREASY YELLOW coating. Supported by cooling light foods (green/mung bean, cucumber, barley, watermelon), avoiding alcohol, smoking and late nights.

source: CCMQ (Wang Qi); Thomson Medical; Delphi PMC8983216
text: Blood-stasis (血瘀质) constitution: poor-circulation type — dull/dark complexion, dark lips, under-eye circles, easy bruising, forgetful, cold-intolerant. Tongue: PURPLE/dusky body, and DISTENDED DARK SUBLINGUAL (under-tongue) VEINS — the strongest objective stasis sign. Supported by circulation-moving foods (hawthorn, black fungus, garlic), staying warm and active without overexertion.

Qi-stagnation (气郁质) constitution: stuck-energy/emotional type — tends thin, introverted, overthinks and worries, chest/abdominal bloating, sighing, mood swings, poor sleep. Tongue: often near-normal, sometimes slightly dark or with redder SIDES (Liver). Supported by movement, social connection, stress relief, and qi-moving foods (radish, kumquat, hawthorn, citrus).

Special/allergic (特禀质) constitution: reactive type — allergies, asthma, hives, sensitivity to foods, pollen, smells or medicines; seasonal flares. Tongue: variable, no single hallmark. Supported by a simple balanced diet, avoiding known triggers, good hygiene and immune-supporting routine.

In WHO ICD-11 Chapter 26 (Traditional Medicine), the Spleen system carries patterns such as Spleen deficiency with qi stagnation (SF72), Spleen deficiency with dampness accumulation (SF79) and Spleen yang deficiency (SF77). Spleen-qi deficiency = the digestive/transport function is weak, producing tiredness, poor appetite, loose stools, and a pale, tooth-marked tongue. This is a standardized pattern name, not a disease diagnosis.

ICD-11 Ch.26 includes 'Dampness-heat encumbering the spleen system pattern' (SF78): damp and heat obstruct the middle burner, giving body heaviness, a bitter sticky mouth, and a red tongue with a greasy yellow coating. A standardized traditional-medicine pattern.

ICD-11 Ch.26 classes environmental/pathogenic factors including DAMPNESS and PHLEGM. Dampness/phlegm patterns present with heaviness, fullness, a swollen tongue and a thick greasy coating — fluids the body isn't transforming. Standardized pattern vocabulary (196 patterns, 150 disorders in the chapter).

ICD-11 Ch.26 includes blood-stasis patterns: blood not moving smoothly, giving fixed/stabbing pains, dark complexion, a purple tongue and distended sublingual veins. Standardized traditional-medicine pattern naming.

ICD-11 Ch.26 includes yin-deficiency patterns (e.g. Spleen yin deficiency SF76 among organ variants): cooling/moistening fluids are depleted so 'empty heat' arises — dryness, night sweats, warm palms, a red tongue with little coat and cracks.

Qi deficiency vs Blood deficiency (both often show a PALE tongue): qi-deficiency tends to a pale, SWOLLEN, TOOTH-MARKED tongue with tiredness, breathlessness and loose stools; blood-deficiency tends to a pale, THIN, dry-ish tongue with dizziness, palpitations, dry skin and poor sleep. Swollen+toothmarks → qi; thin+dry → blood.

Yin-deficiency vs Damp-heat (both can show a RED tongue): yin-deficiency has a red body with LITTLE/PEELED coating and CRACKS (a dry, depleted surface); damp-heat has a red body with a THICK GREASY YELLOW coating (a wet, loaded surface). Scanty-dry coat → yin-def; greasy-yellow coat → damp-heat.

Blood-stasis vs Heat (both can darken the tongue): a PURPLE/dusky body (± dark sublingual veins, purple spots) means blood-stasis; a bright/deep RED body means heat. Purple = stasis, red = heat — colour hue is the tell.

Spleen-qi deficiency vs Yang deficiency (both weak/cold digestion, both pale+swollen+toothmarked): yang-deficiency adds clear COLD signs — a WET tongue, cold limbs, worse with cold, and pale-white. It's qi-deficiency that has progressed to lack of warmth. More wetness and cold → yang; milder → qi.

Yin deficiency vs Blood deficiency (both deficiency, both can be thinnish): blood-deficiency tongue is PALE (undernourished); yin-deficiency tongue is RED with little coat and cracks ('empty heat', dryness). Pale = blood; red-dry = yin.

Damp-heat vs Phlegm-dampness (both greasy-coated): damp-heat is HOT-damp (yellow coat, red body, bitter mouth, oily skin, irritability); phlegm-dampness is COOL/neutral damp (white coat, normal/pale body, heaviness, weight gain). The coating colour and any heat signs decide it.

Modern association (not causation): a thick or greasy tongue coating correlates in studies with digestive/gut-microbiome state and metabolic markers; damp-type tongues are described alongside sluggish digestion and metabolic syndrome. Presented as a correlation of interest, not a diagnosis.

Modern association (not causation): a pale, tooth-marked tongue is repeatedly described alongside low-energy, iron/anaemia-type presentations. In this tradition it maps to Spleen-qi deficiency; framed as an association worth noticing, not a diagnostic claim.

Modern association (not causation): a red tongue with little coating is described alongside states of heat/dehydration or overwork ('running hot'). Traditionally an empty-heat/yin-deficiency picture; an association, not a diagnosis.

Both damp-heat and phlegm-dampness show a thick, greasy tongue coating. The deciding factor is HEAT: a YELLOW coating and/or a RED tongue body means damp-HEAT; a WHITE coating with a normal or pale body, especially if wet, means plain phlegm-dampness (cold-damp).

A swollen tongue occurs in both. If it is also PALE and WET/glossy with a thin white coat, it points to Yang deficiency. If it carries a THICK GREASY coat, it points to phlegm-dampness. Moisture-plus-pale vs thick-greasy is the tell.

id: card:why a pale tongue is ambiguous
text: A pale tongue alone cannot be pinned to one pattern: it appears in Qi, Blood AND Yang deficiency. Co-signs disambiguate — pale+tooth-marks → qi; pale+thin → blood; pale+swollen+wet → yang. Without pulse and symptoms the tongue is genuinely uncertain here; hedge and ask follow-ups.

A tongue tip distinctly redder than the rest reflects heat in the upper body / Heart, commonly linked to stress, poor sleep or restlessness. It is a localized, usually mild sign — it nudges toward heat (yin-deficiency or damp-heat) rather than defining a pattern.

Cracks usually indicate Yin/fluid depletion (a dry, under-nourished surface), especially with a red body and scanty coat. But a single deep central crack can be constitutional or reflect Stomach/Spleen, and some cracks are lifelong and harmless. Read cracks with body colour and coating, not alone.

id: card:greasy coating is texture not colour
text: 'Greasy' describes coating TEXTURE — a sticky, curd-like film hiding the tongue surface — and is the core sign of Dampness/Phlegm. It is independent of coating COLOUR (white vs yellow) and THICKNESS. Papillae can mimic a light film, so a faint greasy read is uncertain.

A wet, glossy tongue suggests fluids not being transformed (cold-damp / Yang-deficiency) and argues AGAINST heat, which dries the surface. A genuinely dry tongue points to Yin/fluid depletion or heat. Photo glare can mimic wetness, so treat moisture as a soft signal.

Distended, dark under-tongue veins are the strongest single objective sign of Blood stasis, but they need lifting the tongue and a second photo, which this tool does not take — so a blood-stasis read here rests only on a purple/dusky body and is correspondingly less certain.

id: card:what the tongue cannot tell you
text: Tongue reading is one of four traditional examinations (looking, listening, asking, pulse). The tongue is informative about coating/damp/heat but weak at separating the deficiency patterns, which need pulse and symptoms. Always frame results as a tendency to explore, never a diagnosis.

Qi stagnation vs Blood stasis (stagnation can deepen into stasis): qi-stagnation is the earlier, functional stage — bloating, sighing, mood swings, a near-normal or slightly dark tongue, sometimes redder Liver SIDES. Blood-stasis is the later, structural stage — fixed/stabbing pain, a PURPLE tongue and dark sublingual veins. Purple/fixed = stasis; distension/mood = stagnation.

Coating colour reads temperature: a WHITE coating leans COLD (or normal if thin); a YELLOW coating leans HEAT (darker/browner yellow = stronger heat). A coat turning from white to yellow suggests cold changing to heat, or an external pattern moving inward.

Coating THICKNESS reads how much has accumulated: a THIN coat (you can see the body through it) is normal or a mild/exterior condition; a THICK coat (hides the body) means more internal accumulation — damp, phlegm or retained food. Thickness is separate from greasiness (texture) and colour.

A ROOTED coat looks like it grows from the tongue (healthy Stomach qi). A ROOTLESS coat sits on top and looks easily wiped off — it suggests weakened Stomach/Kidney qi. A peeled/absent coat is the extreme, pointing to Stomach-Yin damage (red body) or qi/blood depletion (pale body).

A SWOLLEN/enlarged tongue (often with tooth-marks) means the body isn't moving fluids — Dampness/Phlegm, or Spleen/Kidney qi-or-yang deficiency. Pale+wet swollen leans yang-deficiency; thick-greasy-coated swollen leans phlegm-damp; a swollen RED tongue with yellow coat leans damp-heat.
A THIN/small tongue means not enough substance to fill it — Qi and Blood deficiency when pale, or Yin-deficient heat when red and dry. Read with colour: pale-thin = blood/qi; red-thin-dry = yin.

A PURPLE/dusky tongue means blood isn't circulating smoothly — Blood stasis. Reddish-purple leans heat-plus-stasis; bluish-purple leans cold-plus-stasis. Purple spots or dark sublingual veins strengthen the stasis reading.

RED DOTS / raised prickles (enlarged red papillae) mean heat in the blood. Their location matters: dots at the TIP → Heart/Lung (upper-body heat, stress); at the SIDES → Liver/Gallbladder; at the ROOT → lower burner. Often seen with a red body and/or yellow coat in damp-heat.

A PEELED / mirror (glossy, coatless) patch means the Stomach/Kidney fluids that grow the coat are depleted — Yin deficiency when the body is red, or qi/blood depletion when pale. Geographic (map-like) peeling comes and goes with the same meaning, milder.

Cold hands and feet, dislike of cold, wanting warm drinks, and feeling worse after cold food point to a YANG-DEFICIENCY (cold) tendency — on the tongue a pale, swollen, wet body. Warming foods and keeping the core warm are the traditional supports.

Night sweats, dry mouth/throat, warm palms and soles, thirst for cold drinks, and restlessness point to a YIN-DEFICIENCY ('empty heat') tendency — on the tongue a red body with little coat and cracks. Cooling-moistening foods and good rest are the supports.

Bloating after meals, tiredness, poor appetite, loose stools and easy bruising point to a SPLEEN-QI-DEFICIENCY (weak digestion) tendency — on the tongue a pale, tooth-marked body. Warm, well-cooked, easy-to-digest meals and not overeating are the supports.

A heavy or foggy head/limbs, phlegm or mucus, a sticky mouth, puffiness and weight that's hard to shift point to a PHLEGM-DAMPNESS tendency — on the tongue a swollen body with a thick greasy coating. Lighter draining foods and regular movement are the supports.

A bitter or sticky taste, oily skin and acne, body heaviness with warmth, sticky stools and irritability point to a DAMP-HEAT tendency — on the tongue a red body with a greasy yellow coating. Cooling, light foods and avoiding alcohol/late nights are the supports.

Dizziness, palpitations, poor sleep, dry skin/hair, scanty periods and a pale complexion point to a BLOOD-DEFICIENCY (under-nourished) tendency — on the tongue a pale, thin, dry-ish body. Blood-nourishing foods and enough rest are the supports.

Frequent sighing, chest or rib fullness, bloating that comes with mood, irritability or low mood, and a lump-in-throat feeling point to a QI-STAGNATION (stuck energy, stress) tendency — the tongue is often near-normal or slightly dark. Movement, stress relief and qi-moving foods are the supports.
